policy_statement: Produce guidance on integrating mātauranga Māori into adaptive planning and working with mana whenua.
mechanism_description: Produces guidance to help central and local government engage with hapū, iwi and Māori and integrate mātauranga Māori into risk management and adaptation planning, strengthening institutional capacity to adapt.
primary_causal_pathway: Governance capacity-building (knowledge + engagement) → improved climate risk assessment/planning → better adaptation actions → ↓ exposure/vulnerability → ↓ climate impacts
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Government institutions update guidance, processes, and engagement practices to work effectively with hapū/iwi/Māori (mana whenua) and embed mātauranga Māori into adaptation and risk management workflows.  
(2) Mediating process: Improved governance quality—more locally grounded risk identification, better scenario planning, stronger legitimacy and participation, and better coordination—leads to more appropriate, timely, and effective adaptation options being selected and implemented.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced harm from climate hazards (e.g., flooding, coastal erosion, drought, extreme heat) via improved preparedness, planning, and implementation capacity (adaptive capacity and resilience).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is improved adaptation decisions that reduce exposure and vulnerability to climate hazards through stronger, more inclusive institutional risk management capacity.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism strengthens institutional adaptive capacity and improves risk management and adaptation planning, which primarily reduces vulnerability and impacts from climate hazards.  
2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: While improved governance can indirectly influence mitigation or resource efficiency choices, the described causal chain is explicitly about risk management and adaptive planning—core adaptation functions—rather than emissions reduction or efficiency gains.  
3) Edge cases applied: If guidance were primarily used to drive emissions policy (e.g., integrating mātauranga Māori into decarbonization strategy), mitigation could be dominant; however, this canonical mechanism is framed around climate risk management and adaptation planning, making adaptation the primary category.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy whose core mechanism is “Indigenous knowledge integration guidance → stronger institutional adaptive capacity” will primarily act by improving adaptation decision-making and implementation, yielding reduced climate impacts.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any secondary benefits (e.g., improved ecosystem stewardship, mitigation co-benefits, equity outcomes) require policy-specific evidence and are not assigned at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could overlap with Nature-Based Solutions if the integrated mātauranga Māori guidance primarily results in ecosystem restoration as the main adaptation strategy, or with Mitigation if used chiefly for decarbonization governance; under this mechanism’s stated focus (risk management/adaptation planning), Adaptation remains dominant.
key_indicators: "Produce guidance"; "integrating mātauranga Māori into adaptive planning"; "working with mana whenua"; "engage with hapū, iwi and Māori"; "managing risk and planning for adaptation"
co_benefits: Improved Crown–Māori relationships and trust; stronger cultural recognition and governance legitimacy (broader than climate adaptation)
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as an information-type instrument (guidance document), but because it is explicitly about embedding knowledge into planning workflows and engagement practice for adaptation, “planning-strategy” is the better fit.
